records.ety: [OE. sneren, Dan. sn<?/rre to snarl or grin (like a dog); cf. Prov. E. sneer to grin, sner to snort, snert to sneer at. See Snore, v. i.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Sneered (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Sneering.]
usage: Sneer, Scoff, Jeer. The verb to sneer implies to cast contempt indirectly or by covert expressions. To jeer is stronger, and denotes the use of several sarcastic reflections. To scoff is stronger still, implying the use of insolent mockery and derision.

defs: To show contempt by turning up the nose, or by a particular facial expression.
To inssinuate contempt by a covert expression; to speak derisively.
quotes: I could be content to be a little sneared at.
And sneers as learnedly as they, Like females o'er their morning tea.
Midas, exposed to all their jeers, Had lost his art, and kept his ears.
The fop, with learning at defiance, Scoffs at the pedant and science.

defs: To utter with a grimace or contemptuous expression; to utter with a sneer; to say sneeringly; as, to sneer fulsome lies at a person.
To treat with sneers; to affect or move by sneers.
quotes: Nor sneered nor bribed from virtue into shame.

defs: A smile, grin, or contortion of the face, indicative of contempt; an indirect expression or insinuation of contempt.
